ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pomfret food product node; no specific tandoori-marinated pomfret entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a tandoori-specific pomfret entry. A0CJW (pomfret, cooked) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for tandoori pomfret or pomfret as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tandoori pomfret is one whole medium-sized fish weighing approximately 300 g after marinating and grilling, often served with sliced onions, lemon wedges, and mint chutney.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Pomfret (Pampano), raw and cooked values; ICMR-NIN tandoori fish preparation estimates
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides raw pomfret composition and cooked fish values. Tandoori preparation involves marination in yoghurt and spices followed by high-heat tandoor grilling. Values adapted for cooked tandoori state: moisture loss ~30% from raw, protein concentration increases, fat from marinade oil adds ~2–3 g/100g. Sodium elevated due to salt and spice marinade. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked pomfret (FDC ID 175168) and peer-reviewed literature on tandoori fish nutrient retention.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fish and seafood category; analogous estimate for tandoori fish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori pomfret. Pomfret is a lean marine fish with negligible carbohydrate content. GI is estimated at 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for cooked fish and seafood dishes, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 20–40) due to minimal digestible carbohydrate. The small carbohydrate contribution (~2.8 g/100g) comes from the yoghurt-and-spice marinade. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 8.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.5, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade salt and spices (~420 mg/100g; ~1260 mg per whole-fish serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full tandoori pomfret serving can contribute approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-salt marinade or limiting portion to half a fish is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g/100g) and low glycemic index (~30) with negligible glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori pomfret is an excellent protein-rich, low-GI choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its high protein content (22.5 g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability, and the estimated glycemic load of approximately 2.5 per serving has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid profile from pomfret; low saturated fat relative to total fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pomfret provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (approximately 2.1 g/100g) and a low saturated fat fraction (1.8 g/100g), supporting cardiovascular health; however, the elevated sodium from the tandoori marinade warrants caution for patients with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Pomfret is a classic North Indian seafood dish in which a whole pomfret fish is marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture — typically containing red chilli powder, turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and oil — and then cooked at high heat in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a fish with a lightly charred, aromatic exterior, moist and flaky white flesh inside, and a distinctly smoky, tangy, and mildly spiced flavour profile. Pomfret (Pampus argenteus or Parastromateus niger) is a prized marine flatfish widely consumed across South Asia, the Persian Gulf, and Southeast Asia. In Singapore, tandoori pomfret appears on menus at Indian restaurants and is considered a premium seafood dish. Nutritionally, tandoori pomfret is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 22.5 g protein, 7.2 g fat, and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, yielding around 162 kcal per 100 g. It is a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, phosphorus, and omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary clinical consideration is sodium content, which is elevated due to the salt-heavy marinade. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified ingredients.

Is tandoori pomfret good for diabetics?

What fish is used in tandoori pomfret?
What are the ingredients in tandoori pomfret marinade?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for pomfret (cooked) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked pomfret values. No exact tandoori pomfret entry exists in any approved primary source; values reflect a defensible adaptation accounting for marinade ingredients and tandoor cooking moisture loss. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fish category. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct tandoori pomfret entry in any approved primary database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (pomfret, cooked) and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175168, cooked pomfret). Tandoori preparation adds approximately 2–3 g fat/100g from marinade oil and elevates sodium to ~420 mg/100g from salt and spice marinade. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as pomfret is a marine fish with negligible industrial trans fat; any trace ruminant trans fat from yoghurt is below detection threshold (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA sources). 3. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous fish/seafood category from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for tandoori pomfret exists in the approved literature. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = 2.5. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN and USDA cooked pomfret data; these are not -1 as defensible values are available from approved sources. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are closest-parent matches; no exact tandoori pomfret ontology node exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of the accessed date. 6. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded as it is not relevant to tandoori pomfret.
